answers.intake_q1: When did the elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly on your elbow the pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any injury or trauma to the elbow before the pain started?
answers.intake_a3: no, but I was playing tennis when it started
answers.intake_q4: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q5: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arm or hand?
answers.intake_q6: Any difficulty moving your elbow or pain when you try to straighten or bend it?
answers.intake_q7: Any activities or treatments you have tried to relieve the elbow pain?
answers.intake_a7: I iced it the first day
answers.intake_q8: Any fever, chills, or other systemic symptoms since the pain started?
answers.intake_q9: Does the pain worsen with any specific movements or activities, such as gripping or lifting?
answers.intake_a9: worse with tennis and lifting objects
answers.intake_summary: Patient presents with 3 weeks of lateral elbow pain that started during tennis without trauma, no swelling, redness, or neurological symptoms, pain worsens with tennis and lifting, no systemic symptoms. Initial treatment with ice was tried.

note.soap:
S: The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of pain localized to the outside of her elbow. The pain began while playing tennis without any preceding trauma. She reports no swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, tingling, or weakness in the arm or hand. There is no difficulty moving the elbow or pain with bending or straightening. The pain worsens with tennis and lifting objects. She initially tried icing the area on the first day of symptoms. There are no systemic symptoms such as fever or chills.

O: Phone evaluation only; no physical examination or vital signs available.

A: The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by lateral elbow pain exacerbated by repetitive wrist extension and gripping activities such as tennis and lifting. There is no evidence of infection or neurological involvement.

Differential:
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow)
2. Radial tunnel syndrome
3. Osteoarthritis of the elbow
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy. Patient is not pregnant.

P: 
 - First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen at recommended doses. 
 - Alternatives: Topical NSAID gels applied over the lateral elbow to reduce systemic side effects. 
 - Medication safety considerations: Avoid exceeding recommended NSAID doses to prevent gastrointestinal, renal, and cardiovascular adverse effects. Use caution in patients with underlying liver or kidney disease (not present in this patient). Avoid concurrent use of multiple acetaminophen-containing products to prevent liver toxicity. 
 - Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek care if symptoms worsen, if there is new onset swelling, redness, warmth, fever, numbness, tingling, or weakness in the arm or hand, or if pain becomes severe and limits function. 
 - Testing: Not indicated at this time given the typical presentation and absence of red flags. Consider imaging or referral if symptoms persist beyond 6-12 weeks or if neurological symptoms develop.

evidence.title: Tennis elbow - NHS
evidence.snippet: rvices without needing a referral from a GP. Get NHS help for back or joint problems Treatments for tennis elbow Tennis elbow usually gets better after resting your arm for a few weeks, but it can sometimes last longer. A GP may give you anti-inflammatory tablets. They may also refer you for physiotherapy if your symptoms have not improved after trying treat…

evidence.title: content.txt
evidence.snippet: cold pack (or a bag of frozen peas) wrapped in a towel on the affected area for up to 20 minutes every 2 to 3 hours - try doing simple exercises, such as bending and straightening your arm - try wearing a forearm strap or a wrist or elbow brace – you can buy these from pharmacies Important Do not smoke or go near naked flames if you're using ibuprofen gel be…

evidence.title: Elbow Injuries | Elbow Disorders | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. Tendinitis of the elbow is a sports injury , often from playin…

evidence.title: Tendinitis: MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: onnect muscles to bones. They help your muscles move your bones. Tendinitis is the severe swelling of a tendon. Tendinitis usually happens after repeated injury to an area such as the wrist or ankle. It causes pain and soreness around a joint. Some common forms of tendinitis are named after the sports that increase their risk. They include tennis elbow, golf…
